(2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels for electricity/heat/transport and reduced upstream emissions from lower material throughput and improved waste handling (less methane-producing disposal).  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower direct and indirect (Scope 1–2, sometimes Scope 3) GHG emissions across participating businesses and supply chains.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is that more firms adopt lower-carbon operational practices, reducing energy- and fuel-related GHG emissions across sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Adoption of certified environmental practices primarily drives operational changes that reduce fossil energy use and other GHG-intensive activities, yielding net emissions reductions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While certifications can also improve resource efficiency, the mechanism as stated is explicitly oriented to “improved environmental practices that reduce emissions”; the climate-dominant outcome is mitigation via reduced GHGs, not adaptation (hazard exposure) or nature-based sequestration.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some certification schemes may emphasize waste/material efficiency more than direct carbon, but even then the largest climate effect typically remains reduced GHGs from energy/material reductions; if a certification were purely non-climate (e.g., local pollution only), mitigation relevance would weaken, but that is not implied here.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that promotes environmental business certification to drive broader adoption of improved practices has the same main climate lever—firm-level operational changes that lower emissions—so Mitigation is consistent across instances.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether outcomes are mainly building energy, industrial processes, logistics/transport, procurement, or waste reductions depends on the specific certification and participating sectors; that finer assignment requires policy-level evidence and is deferred.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the promoted certification primarily targets non-GHG environmental outcomes (biodiversity, toxics, water) without substantial energy/material change, the mitigation impact could be indirect or minor; if the program is framed mainly as efficiency (same service, less input), it could be co-classified as Resource Efficiency at a secondary level, but primary remains mitigation due to emissions-reduction intent.
key_indicators: “achieve a target of three times as many businesses with environmental certification”; “Increase in the number of companies with environmental certification”; “undertakes to promote these certifications and the companies awarded them”
co_benefits: Improved local air quality; reduced waste generation; enhanced corporate compliance and environmental awareness/branding
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an information/recognition campaign (“promote these certifications and the companies awarded them”), but the explicit quantified objective makes target-setting the best fit; climate impact depends on what the certifications require and uptake by firms.
parent_policy_name: Towards an inclusive economic transition for the region (2030 strategic objectives) to develop shared services/economy of use, second-hand promotion, local currency uptake, certifications, and transition jobs support.

policy_statement: Organise job placement into new professions required for the climate transition in collaboration with regional bodies (Regional Council, COCOF, VGC).
parent_statement: Towards an inclusive economic transition for the region (2030 strategic objectives) to develop shared services/economy of use, second-hand promotion, local currency uptake, certifications, and transition jobs support.
verbatim_text: -  In  collaboration  with  the  Regional  Council,  the  COCOF  and  the  VGC,  organise  job placement in the new professions required for the climate transition.
extraction_rationale: Specific programmatic action (job placement) with named partners.
mechanism_description: The city plans to organize job placement into new climate-transition professions so more skilled workers are available to deliver emissions-reducing projects.
primary_causal_pathway: Workforce/job placement → ↑ implementation capacity for decarbonization projects → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Change in human activity/system: The city creates or coordinates job placement pipelines into climate-transition occupations, increasing the availability and matching of trained workers to climate-related jobs. (2) Mediating process: Reduced labor bottlenecks and improved execution capacity accelerates delivery, scale, and quality of emissions-reducing investments (retrofits, renewable installations, electrification, efficiency upgrades). (3) Climate-relevant outcome: More/earlier decarbonization project completion reduces fossil fuel combustion and associated CO₂ (and potentially other GHGs), lowering net emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is enabling faster and broader implementation of emissions-reducing measures, leading to lower GHG emissions than would occur without the added workforce capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning: (1) Dominant causal pathway: workforce/job placement increases capacity to implement decarbonization actions, which in turn reduces GHG emissions (mitigation outcome). (2) Why this primary over alternatives: While the policy is “governance” and could be framed as enabling or economic development, its core climate effect is indirect but primarily emissions reduction via accelerated deployment; it is not chiefly about reducing exposure to hazards (adaptation), reducing material/energy input for the same service as the primary endpoint (resource efficiency), or enhancing ecosystems (nature-based solutions). (3) Edge cases applied: Some workforce programs could focus on climate resilience jobs (adaptation) or circular economy jobs (resource efficiency); however, this canonical mechanism explicitly targets “implementation capacity for emissions reduction,” making mitigation dominant. (4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose mechanism is job placement → increased capacity to deliver emissions-reducing projects will primarily act through reduced GHG emissions, regardless of city context. (5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: Specific mitigation subtypes (e.g., buildings, transport, energy supply) depend on which sectors the placed workers ultimately serve and are not determined at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Could be misclassified if a specific program primarily staffs adaptation/resilience roles (e.g., flood defense, heat-response services) or focuses mainly on just transition/social outcomes without measurable linkage to emissions-reducing implementation; the canonical mechanism here specifies emissions-reduction capacity, so mitigation remains primary.
key_indicators: “organise job placement”; “new professions required for the climate transition”; “in collaboration with the Regional Council, the COCOF and the VGC”
co_benefits: Employment/unemployment reduction; skills development and social inclusion; regional economic development
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a “planning-strategy” (workforce planning/coordination), but the operative instrument is delivery of a workforce/job-placement program rather than a plan or target.
parent_policy_name: Towards an inclusive economic transition for the region (2030 strategic objectives) to develop shared services/economy of use, second-hand promotion, local currency uptake, certifications, and transition jobs support.

section_header: Children and young people are key parties in the climate change transition
policy_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text:
## 2030 Strategic objectives

## 1. Extending and strengthening the City of Brussels eco-crèches model
...
2. An exemplary school environment
...
## Raising awareness and equipping young people to tackle climate change issues
extraction_rationale: Thematic chapter with structured objective lists (infrastructure/mobility/waste/awareness; school environment); umbrella for sub-policies.
mechanism_description: The city plans to expand eco-crèches and improve school environments while raising awareness and equipping young people on climate issues, supporting pro-climate behavior and long-term institutional capacity for climate action.
primary_causal_pathway: Education/awareness + capacity building → behavior/decision change + improved policy implementation → ↓ energy/material demand and other emitting activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed human activity/system: The city expands climate-oriented early-childhood programs (eco-crèches), improves school environments, and delivers youth climate education/awareness, increasing climate literacy, norms, and skills among students, staff, and institutions.  
2) Mediating process: Increased knowledge and pro-social norms shift individual and organizational choices (e.g., energy conservation, low-carbon mobility, waste reduction, support for climate policies) and builds administrative/educational capacity that improves planning, procurement, and program delivery.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Aggregate reductions in emissions over time from lower-carbon behaviors and more effective implementation of mitigation measures (with potential secondary benefits like resilience awareness and preparedness).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is downstream reductions in GHG emissions via behavior change and stronger implementation of mitigation actions enabled by improved climate literacy and institutional capacity.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education/awareness and capacity building primarily drive behavior and institutional decision changes that reduce emissions indirectly but at scale over time.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “eco-crèches” and school environment improvements can include resource-efficiency measures or adaptation education, the mechanism as stated centers on awareness/equipping youth to enable pro-climate action—most commonly leveraged for emissions reduction and broader mitigation policy uptake.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific program is explicitly oriented to disaster preparedness/heat-health behaviors, the dominant pathway could become Adaptation; if the intervention is mainly school energy retrofits, it could be Resource Efficiency. Those are policy-level specifics not inherent to this mechanism.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that is fundamentally climate education/awareness/capacity building shares the same primary causal logic of enabling mitigation through behavior and institutional capability, even if the exact downstream actions vary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Sub-classification (e.g., mitigation via energy conservation vs. waste vs. mobility; or adaptation co-benefits) requires evidence from the конкрет policy design and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Education/awareness is an enabling mechanism with indirect impacts; classification hinges on the most common dominant outcome (mitigation via behavior/policy uptake). If the policy text or implementation evidence shows the primary intent/outcome is hazard preparedness (Adaptation) or facility efficiency upgrades (Resource Efficiency), reclassification may be warranted at the policy level.
key_indicators: “2030 Strategic objectives”; “Extending and strengthening … eco-crèches model”; “An exemplary school environment”; “Raising awareness and equipping young people to tackle climate change issues”
co_benefits: Potential education quality improvements; healthier indoor environments for children; waste reduction and local environmental quality benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be coded as an infrastructure-investment if the “exemplary school environment” section specifies funded retrofits/physical upgrades; based on the provided excerpt, the dominant signal is strategy + awareness/education rather than explicit capital works or mandates.
primary_gate_reason: no Adaptation outcome token found in source; flagging for review
rescue_reasoning: Keep because climate relevance is explicit in the verbatim text: “Raising awareness and equipping young people to tackle climate change issues.” The described intervention matches the canonical mechanism of climate education/awareness leading to pro-climate behavior and institutional capacity. Reclassify because the content does not specify direct mitigation outcomes (e.g., emissions reductions) and is better characterized as capacity/education rather than Mitigation.

section_header: 2030 Strategic objectives - Extending and strengthening the City of Brussels eco-crèches model
policy_statement: Eco-crèches: generalise washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches; continue replacing plastic crockery with durable materials in 100% of eco-crèches; and use eco-responsible care/cleaning approaches across 100% of eco-crèches.
parent_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text:
-  ... generalising the use of washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches.
-  Extend the use of washable nappies to 100% of eco-crèches.
-  Continuing the gradual replacement of plastic crockery ... in 100% of eco-crèches...
-  Care  and  cleaning  products: ... use eco-responsible products/diaper-changing techniques in 100% of the city's eco-crèches.
extraction_rationale: Multiple explicit 100% implementation commitments for eco-crèche practices.
mechanism_description: The city expands reusable nappies, replaces plastic crockery with durable materials, and uses eco-responsible care/cleaning practices in eco-crèches to reduce single-use waste and the emissions associated with producing and disposing of those materials.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (childcare/food service) → ↓ material throughput (single-use products) → ↓ embodied energy & upstream GHG emissions (and ↓ waste-treatment emissions)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Childcare facilities and city programs substitute reusable nappies and durable tableware for disposable items and adopt lower-waste procurement/cleaning routines.  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Reduced demand for virgin material extraction, manufacturing, and transportation of disposable goods; reduced waste generation lowers collection/processing needs and landfill/incineration loads.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions, dominated by avoided production (embodied carbon) and supplemented by avoided disposal emissions and operational energy in waste management.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is avoided upstream GHG emissions from manufacturing and supplying fewer single-use products due to waste prevention and reuse.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Deliver the same childcare and meal service with fewer single-use materials, cutting embodied energy and lifecycle GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While mitigation occurs, the mechanism acts mainly by reducing resource and material throughput (a classic resource-efficiency lever) rather than directly controlling an emission source (e.g., landfill methane capture) or managing climate hazards (adaptation) or enhancing ecosystems (NBS).  
3) Edge cases applied: If reuse systems significantly increase laundering energy/water, the net benefit depends on implementation (energy source, load efficiency), but the intended and typical dominant effect remains reduced lifecycle emissions via waste prevention.  
4) Applies to ALL instances of this mechanism: Any policy framed as “waste prevention and reuse → reduced waste generation” shares the same primary resource-efficiency pathway of avoided material production and disposal.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a given policy’s secondary classification leans more toward upstream manufacturing avoidance vs. downstream disposal avoidance requires policy-level evidence (materials displaced, laundering/cleaning energy, waste treatment baseline).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Potential rebound/offset if reusables require energy- or water-intensive laundering using high-carbon energy, or if durable goods are over-specified (high embodied carbon) and not used enough cycles; classification remains Resource Efficiency because the mechanism’s core is reducing material throughput for the same service.
key_indicators: “generalising the use of washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches”; “Extend the use of washable nappies to 100% of eco-crèches”; “replacement of plastic crockery with durable materials in 100% of eco-crèches”; “use eco-responsible products/diaper-changing techniques in 100% of the city’s eco-crèches”
co_benefits: Reduced solid waste and litter; potential reductions in plastic pollution; improved indoor environmental quality from “eco-responsible” cleaning products; possible cost savings over time from reusables.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a procurement requirement (durable crockery, eco-responsible products) or operational mandate for municipal facilities; classified as target-setting because the text is framed as quantified coverage objectives (“100%”) rather than explicit purchasing rules or legal obligations. Mention of “eco-responsible” cleaning is ambiguous (could include toxics reduction more than climate).
parent_policy_name: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
rescue_reasoning: Climate screen is explicit_self, so the row is climate-relevant even if the primary gate missed an outcome token. The measures are clearly resource-efficiency / waste-prevention actions: the verbatim text commits to 'generalising the use of washable nappies in 100% of eco-crèches' (replacing disposable nappies with reusable ones) and 'replacement of plastic crockery with durable materials' (durable/reusable alternatives to plastic items). These directly align with the stated mechanism waste_prevention_and_reuse_program → waste_generation_reduction. Therefore the deterministic failure ('no Resource Efficiency outcome token found') is a false negative and the row should be kept.

section_header: 2030 Strategic objectives - Extending and strengthening the City of Brussels eco-crèches model
policy_statement: Eco-crèches: strengthen food sustainability with 100% organic, local and seasonal food; increase non-meat meals; reduce waste; and extend Good Food certification to all host environments.
parent_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text: -  Strengthen food sustainability by offering 100% organic, local (including from Brussels agriculture)  and  seasonal  food,  increasing  the  frequency  of  non-meat  meals  and drastically  reducing  waste.  On  this  basis,  extend  Good  Food  certification  to  all  host environments.
extraction_rationale: Specific operational standards (100% organic/local/seasonal) and certification extension.
mechanism_description: It strengthens food sustainability by shifting meals toward organic/local/seasonal and more non-meat options while reducing food waste, which lowers food-related greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Food procurement & consumption shift + ↓ food waste → ↓ agricultural/land-use + supply-chain emissions (and ↓ landfill methane) → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Changed activity/system: Public/institutional food procurement and meal design shift toward plant-forward menus and (often) organic/local/seasonal sourcing, alongside operational changes that prevent and reduce food waste (planning, portioning, storage, donation/redistribution).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological processes: Reduced demand for emissions-intensive foods (notably beef/lamb and dairy) lowers enteric methane, manure-related emissions, feed production emissions, and associated land-use change pressures; reducing wasted food avoids the upstream emissions embedded in producing/transporting food that is not eaten and can also avoid methane from anaerobic decomposition if less food ends up landfilled.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in lifecycle GHG emissions attributable to the food system (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O), producing mitigation benefits.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced food-system lifecycle GHG emissions (especially from lower ruminant meat demand and avoided food waste), leading to net emissions reductions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Changing procurement/menus and cutting food waste reduces lifecycle food-related GHG emissions (agriculture + supply chain + avoided waste), which is a direct mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “local/seasonal/organic” and waste prevention can improve resource efficiency, the mechanism’s main climate-relevant effect is emissions reduction rather than primarily reducing energy/material inputs for the same service or reducing climate hazard exposure.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a policy emphasized only reducing waste disposal impacts (e.g., composting) without preventing waste, emissions effects could shift toward waste-management mitigation; if it focused on nutrition/health without measurable procurement/waste changes, climate effects would be incidental. Under this canonical mechanism, the intent and effect remain emissions reduction from food-system changes.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that is “plant-forward procurement + food waste reduction” is inherently aimed at lowering food-system emissions (especially from reduced animal-product demand and avoided wasted production), so Mitigation is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specifics (e.g., whether benefits are dominated by reduced ruminant consumption vs waste prevention vs procurement standards like organic/local) require policy-level evidence and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If “local/seasonal/organic” procurement is implemented in ways that increase energy-intensive production (e.g., heated greenhouses) or transport/storage, net emissions benefits could vary; however, plant-forward shifts and waste prevention typically dominate the mitigation effect within this mechanism.
key_indicators: “offering 100% organic, local … and seasonal food”; “increasing the frequency of non-meat meals”; “drastically reducing waste”; “extend Good Food certification to all host environments”
co_benefits: Improved public health/nutrition; support for local agriculture; reduced air/water pollution from agriculture; biodiversity/soil benefits (potentially via organic practices); cost savings from reduced waste.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Good Food certification” could be treated as an information/certification instrument, but here it is used as a condition/requirement for institutional food environments, making procurement-requirement the best fit.
parent_policy_name: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).

section_header: 2030 Strategic objectives - Extending and strengthening the City of Brussels eco-crèches model
policy_statement: Eco-crèches: provide at least one eco-training per year for all service staff and at least one per year for all childcare staff; and organise at least one eco-crèche-related activity per year with parents in 100% of eco-crèches.
parent_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text: -  Organisation of at least one eco-training session per year for all service staff, organisation of at least one eco-training session per year for all childcare staff... and organisation of at least one activity linked to the  eco-crèche  project per  year  with parents  in  100%  of  eco-crèches...
extraction_rationale: Quantified training/activity frequency requirements with full coverage.
mechanism_description: The policy organizes annual eco-training for staff and eco-crèche activities with parents to build knowledge and encourage lower-impact daily practices that reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Education/engagement → behavior & practice change → ↓ energy/material use & ↓ high-carbon activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human activity/system changed: staff, children, and parents’ routines and operational practices in facilities (and sometimes at home) via trainings and engagement activities. (2) Mediating process: increased knowledge, salience, and social norms lead to adoption of lower-carbon actions (e.g., turning off equipment, reducing waste, choosing lower-carbon transport/food, improved recycling), lowering fossil energy demand and emissions-intensive consumption. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: net reduction in CO₂ and other GHG emissions attributable to reduced energy use, reduced waste-related emissions, and lower-carbon consumption choices.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions due to education-driven changes in everyday practices and operational behaviors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: education and engagement are used to drive behavior change, which in turn reduces energy/material use or shifts choices to lower-carbon options, yielding ↓ GHG emissions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while “resource efficiency” can be an intermediate outcome (using less energy/material for the same service), the climate-relevant endpoint emphasized by the mechanism is emissions reduction; the pathway is not primarily about adapting to hazards or enhancing ecosystems.  
3) Edge cases applied: some education programs may focus on preparedness (adaptation) or ecological stewardship (nature-based), but this canonical mechanism explicitly targets “behavior change emissions reduction,” keeping mitigation as primary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy that fits this mechanism—education/engagement explicitly intended to reduce emissions via behavior change—shares the same dominant mitigation pathway even if the specific behaviors differ by context (workplace, school, community).  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: any secondary tagging (e.g., whether the main lever is energy conservation vs waste reduction vs mobility choices) requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Education/engagement can be a weak or indirect lever if not paired with enabling infrastructure; however, classification is based on intended dominant causal pathway (behavior change → emissions reduction). If a specific program is primarily resilience/preparedness education, it would belong to Adaptation, but that is outside this canonical mechanism definition.
key_indicators: "Organisation of at least one eco-training session per year"; "for all service staff"; "for all childcare staff"; "organisation of at least one activity linked to the eco-crèche project per year with parents"; "in 100% of eco-crèches"
co_benefits: Environmental education and awareness; reduced waste/litter; potential public health co-benefits from improved indoor practices and cleaner environments; community engagement
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a program/performance requirement for maintaining an “eco-crèche” designation, but the text reads primarily as a mandatory training/activity frequency requirement rather than a quantified performance standard.
parent_policy_name: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).

section_header: 2030 Strategic objectives - An exemplary school environment
policy_statement: School infrastructure: install water fountains in all school buildings and install rainwater recovery systems in schools with vegetable gardens; develop sustainable water management initiatives in schools.
parent_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text:
-  Develop sustainable water management initiatives in schools (drinking fountains, water bottles, rainwater tanks, infiltration and flooding zones in courtyards, etc.).
- o The  City  Council  is  committed  to  installing  rainwater  recovery  systems  in schools with vegetable gardens.
- o The City Council is committed to equipping all its school buildings with water fountains.
extraction_rationale: Concrete infrastructure commitments with clear scope.
mechanism_description: The City Council is committed to equipping school buildings with drinking fountains and adding rainwater recovery and infiltration measures so schools can rely less on potable water and better manage stormwater and flooding.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence_quote not substring-grounded in source
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard (heavier rainfall) → stormwater retained/infiltrated/reused → ↓ runoff peak flows & sewer overload → ↓ flood exposure and impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in system/activity: Schools are retrofitted with rainwater recovery (cisterns/barrels) and infiltration/green stormwater measures (e.g., soakaways, permeable areas, infiltration trenches), alongside drinking fountains to reduce reliance on bottled/potable supply for some uses.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Capturing rainfall and increasing infiltration/temporary storage reduces the volume and rate of stormwater entering drains and waterways, lowering peak discharge and combined sewer overflow risk; reuse also substitutes some potable demand.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved resilience to intensified precipitation events (less localized flooding and overflow), with a secondary co-benefit of reduced energy use and emissions from potable water treatment/pumping where applicable.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced flood and stormwater damage by lowering runoff volumes/peak flows and increasing on-site retention/infiltration during extreme precipitation.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Managing stormwater through capture/infiltration primarily reduces exposure to flooding from heavier rainfall—an adaptation outcome.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While potable water demand reduction can save energy/embedded emissions (resource efficiency/mitigation co-benefit), those savings are typically smaller and highly dependent on local water system energy intensity; flood-risk reduction is the more direct and generally larger climate-relevant effect for stormwater management measures.  
3) Edge cases applied: In arid regions or very energy-intensive desalination systems, potable demand reduction could dominate; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly includes “stormwater management … flood mitigation,” which anchors the dominant effect in resilience to rainfall extremes.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism includes stormwater capture/infiltration for flood mitigation, which consistently maps to adaptation via reduced runoff and flood exposure, even if potable-demand impacts vary.  
5) Note on secondary subtypes: Any mitigation/resource-efficiency co-benefits (reduced treatment/pumping energy, reduced bottled water) should be assessed at the individual policy level; only the primary category is assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If implemented solely for water-supply substitution (with negligible flood benefit) or in contexts where potable water is extremely energy/carbon intensive (e.g., desalination-dominant supply), the dominant effect could shift toward Resource Efficiency/Mitigation; otherwise, flood-risk reduction remains primary.
key_indicators: "installing rainwater recovery systems"; "equipping all its school buildings with water fountains"; "rainwater tanks"; "infiltration and flooding zones in courtyards"; "Develop sustainable water management initiatives in schools"
co_benefits: Reduced potable water demand; reduced bottled water use/waste; potential energy/GHG reductions from lower water treatment and pumping; educational/behavioral benefits for students
instance_edge_case_notes: Drinking fountains alone are not necessarily climate-specific, but in combination with rainwater recovery/infiltration measures and the stated “sustainable water management initiatives,” the overall package is best classified as direct adaptation-focused infrastructure investment.
parent_policy_name: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).

section_header: 2030 Strategic objectives - An exemplary school environment
policy_statement: School mobility: ensure 100% of schools have a School Travel Plan by 2030; support primary schools with cycle passes; and provide cycle parking in all renovation/construction projects.
parent_statement: Children and young people transition strategy: extend and strengthen the City of Brussels eco-crèches model and implement exemplary school environment and youth education measures (2030 strategic objectives).
verbatim_text:
-  Continue  to  develop  School  Travel  Plans  within  our  schools...
- o 100% of schools will have a School Travel Plan by 2030, with the support of the regional council.
- o The city is committed to supporting all its primary schools by organising cycle passes for pupils.
... In all renovation and construction projects, the city undertakes to provide indoor and/or outdoor cycle parking.
extraction_rationale: Quantified school coverage target with supporting measures.
mechanism_description: The city will develop School Travel Plans and support cycling (cycle passes and cycle parking) to increase low-carbon school travel and reduce transport emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Mobility system change (↑ transit access + ↑ multimodal connectivity) → modal shift (↓ single-occupancy driving, ↓ VKT) → ↓ fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Cities invest in and redesign mobility access (mobility hubs, park-and-ride, bike/scooter links, integrated transfers) to make transit and shared/active modes easier and more reliable than driving alone.  
2) Mediating physical process: Reduced internal-combustion vehicle use lowers gasoline/diesel combustion (and associated upstream fuel-cycle emissions); in some cases, congestion relief can further reduce per-km emissions, though rebound can offset.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Net reduction in transport CO₂e emissions through lower vehicle travel and substitution toward lower-emitting passenger-km modes.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced tailpipe (and upstream) GHG emissions from fewer car trips and/or shorter car travel due to a modal shift to lower-carbon transport.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Improve multimodal connectivity to enable modal shift away from single-occupancy vehicles, leading to lower fuel combustion and lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While such hubs can improve accessibility (a co-benefit) and sometimes reduce energy use per trip (resource efficiency), the defining and largest climate effect across policies sharing this mechanism is emissions mitigation via reduced car use.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “mobility hub” primarily induces additional driving (e.g., large park-and-ride generating new trips) or is implemented mainly for resilience/evacuation, the dominant effect could weaken or shift; however, the stated mechanism explicitly targets modal shift to sustainable transport and emissions reduction.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is shifting trips from single-occupancy driving to transit/active/shared modes will primarily operate through transport emissions reductions, making Mitigation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy’s realized impact is large, and whether it also qualifies for secondary labels (e.g., air quality, equity, congestion, adaptation co-benefits), depends on policy-specific design and evidence and is not assigned at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Park-and-ride elements can create induced driving or shift trips from active modes to car-to-transit, reducing net benefits; outcomes depend on parking supply/pricing, transit frequency, land-use context, and whether buses/rail are low-carbon. If transit vehicles are diesel and poorly utilized, emissions reductions may be smaller, but the intended dominant pathway remains mitigation via modal shift.
key_indicators: “100% of schools will have a School Travel Plan by 2030”; “support…by organising cycle passes for pupils”; “in all renovation and construction projects…the city undertakes to provide…cycle parking”
co_benefits: Improved road safety around schools; reduced local air pollution and noise; increased physical activity/health; reduced congestion at school drop-off/pick-up times.

section_header: Transforming the public authority to ensure a successful transition
policy_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text:
## 2030 Strategic objectives

## 1. Human resources management to meet future challenges

## 2. The City Council's finances and tax policy, tools for transition

## 3. Making public procurement more sustainable

## 4. Climate planning and the City Council's internal monitoring methodology

## 5. Technology at the service of climate planning
extraction_rationale: Thematic chapter with multiple numbered strategic objective lists; umbrella for sub-policies.
mechanism_description: The city plans to improve internal climate planning, monitoring methodology, and supporting technology so it has better data and tools to guide climate action over time.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [ecosystem, habitat, tree, trees, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Monitoring & analytics capacity → better risk information & decision-making → improved planning/early action → ↓ climate vulnerability (↓ exposure/sensitivity)
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city upgrades internal monitoring systems, datasets, methodologies (e.g., inventories, models, surveys), and digital tools, and builds staff/process capacity to use them in ongoing planning.  
(2) Mediating process: Improved measurement, tracking, and analysis of climate hazards, vulnerabilities, and performance indicators reduces uncertainty and supports timely identification of risks and evaluation of options.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: More effective and targeted adaptation plans and interventions are selected and implemented, leading to reduced vulnerability and improved preparedness for climate impacts.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improved climate risk management by generating better information and tools that enable more effective adaptation planning and response.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s direct effect is informational/institutional—better monitoring and analytics that most strongly improve climate risk identification and adaptive decision-making, leading to reduced vulnerability.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While similar systems can also support mitigation tracking (e.g., emissions inventories) and resource efficiency management, the mechanism as stated (“adaptation planning support” and internal climate planning tools) most directly maps to adaptive capacity and vulnerability reduction rather than immediate emissions reduction or efficiency gains.  
3) Edge cases applied: If the monitoring system is explicitly centered on GHG accounting/MRV for emissions caps, it could be mitigation-dominant; if centered on energy/water efficiency KPI management, it could be resource-efficiency-dominant. Here, the canonical mechanism explicitly frames adaptation planning support, so Adaptation is selected.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core is improving environmental/climate monitoring and methods to support adaptation planning primarily functions by increasing adaptive capacity and improving vulnerability-reducing decisions, even if it has co-benefits for mitigation tracking.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Whether a specific policy primarily supports hazards/early warning, infrastructure resilience planning, or mitigation MRV is policy-evidence-specific and should be assigned at the individual policy level, not at the mechanism level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some representative examples (e.g., detailed building modeling to support emissions reduction) could be mitigation-enabling rather than adaptation-enabling; classification here follows the canonical mechanism framing (adaptation planning support) and treats mitigation support as a potential co-benefit/secondary pathway.
key_indicators: “2030 Strategic objectives”; “Climate planning”; “internal monitoring methodology”; “Technology at the service of climate planning”; “climate monitoring”; “Green IT”; “strengthen HR capacity”; “sustainable procurement”
co_benefits: Mitigation/energy and resource-efficiency co-benefits via “Green IT” and “making public procurement more sustainable”; broader fiscal/governance modernization via “finances and tax policy.”
instance_edge_case_notes: This is a bundle of internal governance objectives (HR, finance/tax, procurement, monitoring, IT). If the procurement or tax components were specified as binding rules or budgeted programs, they could shift classification toward procurement-requirement, pricing-mechanism, or incentive-program; however, in the provided text they remain high-level strategic objectives, so planning-strategy is most fitting.

policy_statement: Train, empower and raise awareness of climate issues among City Council staff by function/department, and inform all new employees of relevant climate issues upon starting work.
parent_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text:
## 1. Human resources management to meet future challenges

-  The departments are developing skills to implement actions that meet the objectives of the climate plan:
- o Training,  empowering  and  raising  awareness  of  climate  issues  among  City Council staff...
- o All new employees are informed of the climate issues relevant to their role as soon as they start work.
extraction_rationale: Specific institutional capacity-building measures.
mechanism_description: The city provides training and awareness-raising on climate issues for staff and informs new employees at onboarding, strengthening internal capacity to implement climate actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Workforce capability ↑ → climate plans/programs implemented more effectively → ↓ community/municipal GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Human activity/system changed: municipal workforce skills, awareness, and internal procedures/decision quality are strengthened via training, onboarding content, and departmental empowerment. (2) Mediating process: improved institutional capacity increases the rate, fidelity, and coordination of implementing climate policies (e.g., energy, transport, buildings, procurement), reducing implementation gaps and improving program performance. (3) Climate-relevant outcome: indirect but material reductions in GHG emissions due to more effective execution of mitigation measures (with potential co-benefits for adaptation, depending on what actions are implemented).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is improved institutional capacity that increases implementation and effectiveness of downstream climate actions (most often emissions-reduction measures).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: capacity-building → better implementation of climate actions → most commonly leads to stronger delivery of emissions-reducing interventions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the mechanism itself does not directly change physical infrastructure, ecosystems, resource intensity, or hazard exposure; its largest climate effect is mediated through downstream measures, which in most city climate action contexts are mitigation-focused (energy/buildings/transport).  
3) Edge cases applied: training could primarily support adaptation (e.g., emergency management) or resource efficiency (e.g., circular procurement) depending on curriculum and linked mandates; however, those are not guaranteed by the mechanism description itself.  
4) Confirm primary applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: all such policies raise staff climate capability; the consistently dominant climate-relevant consequence is improved implementation capacity that typically enables emissions-reduction delivery across municipal functions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether the enabled actions are mitigation vs adaptation-heavy (and the magnitude) requires policy-level evidence about the specific programs, departments, and implementation mandates tied to the training.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: This is an enabling governance mechanism with indirect climate impacts; classification is sensitive to what downstream actions the training is designed to implement (could shift to Adaptation if focused on risk management/emergency preparedness, or to Resource Efficiency if focused on circularity/efficiency operations). If your taxonomy allows an “Enabling/Capacity-building” meta-category, that would be a better primary label than Mitigation.
key_indicators: "Training, empowering and raising awareness of climate issues among City Council staff"; "All new employees are informed of the climate issues relevant to their role"
co_benefits: None
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning-strategy (internal governance/capacity-building) if the taxonomy includes internal management measures there; however the dominant mechanism is staff information/training rather than formal planning or mandates.
primary_gate_reason: no Adaptation outcome token found in source; flagging for review
parent_policy_name: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
rescue_reasoning: Climate relevance is explicit in the source: "Training, empowering and raising awareness of climate issues among City Council staff" and "All new employees are informed of the climate issues relevant to their role". However, the text describes institutional capacity-building (training/onboarding) rather than a direct mitigation outcome or emissions-reduction action, so the assigned primary category 'Mitigation' is likely incorrect. Given the parent context of strengthening HR capacity to implement the climate plan, the most fitting primary category is Adaptation (governance/institutional capacity).

policy_statement: Apply for maximum available European/regional/FWB subsidies for climate-related investments and strengthen/diversify ethical, socially responsible and sustainable investments.
parent_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text:
## 2. The City Council's finances and tax policy, tools for transition

-  For  its  investments  linked  to  climate  objectives,  the  City  Council  is  applying  for  a maximum of European, regional and FWB subsidies, etc.
-  Strengthen and diversify ethical, socially responsible and sustainable investments.
extraction_rationale: Explicit financial/resource mobilisation commitments.
mechanism_description: The City Council applies for available subsidies and strengthens sustainable investments to increase funding for climate-related investments, accelerating delivery of climate actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Finance mobilization → ↑ low-carbon investment deployment → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The city changes its capital-raising and budgetary practice by applying for external subsidies and redirecting/expanding ethical and sustainable investment vehicles to increase funds for climate actions. (2) Increased and lower-cost capital accelerates procurement, construction, and uptake of low-carbon infrastructure and programs (e.g., building retrofits, clean energy, low-emission transport), shortening time-to-implementation and expanding scale. (3) Earlier and larger deployment reduces fossil energy use and other emitting activities, yielding lower citywide GHG emissions over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is accelerating and scaling implementation of emissions-reducing projects by increasing available capital for climate investments.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: finance mobilization increases the rate/scale of implementing low-carbon measures, leading to lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: while the mechanism is “enabling,” its largest climate effect across policies is downstream emissions reduction from funded decarbonization projects; it is not inherently about reducing hazard exposure (Adaptation), reducing input use for the same service as the direct mechanism (Resource Efficiency), or ecosystem-mediated outcomes (Nature-Based Solutions).  
3) Edge cases applied: some financed investments could be adaptation-heavy (e.g., flood defenses) or nature-based (e.g., urban greening); however, the mechanism as stated is generic climate-investment acceleration and is most commonly and materially linked to mitigation outcomes in city climate finance programs.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy sharing this mechanism (mobilize subsidies/investment capacity to accelerate climate investments) has its primary climate relevance through enabling faster/larger implementation of climate actions, most consequentially mitigation actions, even if the funded portfolio can vary.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether a specific policy instance is chiefly financing mitigation vs adaptation vs nature-based projects requires policy-level evidence about the funded investment mix and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the mobilized finance is explicitly restricted to resilience/adaptation infrastructure or ecosystem restoration, the dominant pathway could shift to Adaptation or Nature-Based Solutions; absent such restriction, mitigation-enabled acceleration is the typical dominant effect.
key_indicators: “applying for a maximum of European, regional and FWB subsidies”; “investments linked to climate objectives”; “strengthen and diversify ethical, socially responsible and sustainable investments”
co_benefits: Improved municipal fiscal capacity; reputational/governance benefits from ethical investment practices
instance_edge_case_notes: Instrument type could also be interpreted as a “planning-strategy” (financial strategy) since it is an internal budgeting/financing commitment rather than a public-facing incentive offered to others; classified as incentive-program because the core operational lever is the use of grants/subsidies to fund climate investments.
parent_policy_name: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
rescue_reasoning: Verbatim text explicitly links finance to climate action: “For its investments linked to climate objectives, the City Council is applying for a maximum of European, regional and FWB subsidies.” This is a climate-finance mobilization/acceleration mechanism even without an explicit mitigation outcome token (e.g., CO2). It also adds “Strengthen and diversify ethical, socially responsible and sustainable investments,” which supports sustainable (often climate-aligned) investment practices. Climate screen verdict is explicit_self, so the row is climate-relevant and should be retained under the existing finance mechanism; no reclassification is necessary.

policy_statement: Use taxation to encourage virtuous behaviours contributing to the transition and discourage harmful behaviours.
parent_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text: -  The  City  Council  is  committed  to  using  taxation  as  a  tool  to  encourage  virtuous behaviour  that  contributes  to  the  transition  and  to  discourage  behaviour  that  is harmful to the climate.
extraction_rationale: Explicit commitment to use fiscal policy as a lever.
mechanism_description: The City Council commits to using taxation to encourage behaviors that support the transition and discourage behaviors that are harmful to the climate, shifting activity in ways that reduce emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Price signal (tax/fee) → behavior & investment shift → ↓ fossil fuel use/other emitting activities → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Changes: Households, businesses, and/or municipal operations face higher costs for emissions-intensive choices (e.g., fossil fuels, high-emitting travel, carbon-intensive goods) and/or relatively lower costs for low-carbon options.  
(2) Process: Economic incentives drive substitution, reduced consumption, efficiency improvements, and technology switching; producers may also reduce emissions to avoid taxes.  
(3) Outcome: Net decrease in CO₂e emissions (and sometimes co-pollutants) across covered sectors.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions due to taxed activities becoming more expensive, shifting demand and investment toward lower-carbon alternatives.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: fiscal pricing changes behavior and investment, which primarily lowers emissions from energy, transport, waste, and consumption.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: while taxation can also raise revenue (enabling measures) or reduce resource use (efficiency), the mechanism as stated is explicitly to discourage climate-harmful behaviors and encourage transition-supporting ones—its main climate effect is emissions reduction (Mitigation).  
3) Edge cases applied: if a tax is designed mainly to fund resilience projects or to manage climate risk exposure, adaptation could become primary; if it primarily reduces material throughput independent of emissions, resource efficiency could be primary. Those are not the dominant intent/effect described here.  
4) Applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any policy whose core mechanism is “tax/price signal to shift behavior away from high-emitting activities” has mitigation as the dominant climate pathway, regardless of the taxed domain (fuel, congestion, waste, etc.).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence (e.g., transport pricing vs. building energy taxes vs. waste levies; revenue recycling design; equity provisions).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the “taxation” is not tied to emissions-intensive activities (e.g., a general tax increase) or is primarily a revenue-raising measure with emissions reductions incidental, this mechanism label may be over-broad; confirm the tax base is emissions-linked or strongly correlated with GHG outcomes. If revenues are earmarked for adaptation/resilience, mitigation may still be primary but adaptation can be a significant co-benefit.
key_indicators: “using taxation as a tool”; “encourage virtuous behaviour”; “discourage behaviour that is harmful to the climate”
co_benefits: Potential air quality and public health improvements; potential congestion/noise reduction (if transport-related taxes); revenue generation for public services—mentioned implicitly via taxation but not specified.
instance_edge_case_notes: The specific tax base/design (e.g., carbon tax, fuel tax, congestion charge, differentiated property tax) is not specified, but the instrument type is unambiguously a taxation-based pricing mechanism aimed at climate mitigation.
parent_policy_name: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.

policy_statement: Implement sustainable procurement by ensuring contracts include ecological, social and ethical clauses; develop a sustainable procurement strategy; monitor contract effectiveness; and train/raise awareness among purchasers/users.
parent_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text:
## 3. Making public procurement more sustainable

-  Public procurement contracts meet sustainable procurement criteria, incorporating ecological, social and ethical clauses.
- o The City Council, via the Procurement & Facilities department, will develop a sustainable  procurement  strategy...
- o The  city  is  committed  to  monitoring  its  public  procurement  contracts  to measure their effectiveness...
- o Public purchasers and/or users will be trained or made aware...
extraction_rationale: Concrete procurement policy actions and institutional commitments.
mechanism_description: The city will develop and apply sustainable procurement criteria in public contracts, monitor effectiveness, and train purchasers so purchasing shifts toward lower-impact goods and services, reducing lifecycle emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [ghg, greenhouse gas, greenhouse gases, emission, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Procurement demand + contract set → supplier practices/product mix shift → ↓ life-cycle (Scope 3) GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: Public agencies, anchor institutions, and large organizations modify purchasing decisions and vendor selection using sustainability standards (e.g., embodied-carbon limits, recycled/biobased content, efficient equipment, low-GWP refrigerants, low-carbon logistics, EPD reporting).  
(2) Mediating physical/industrial process: Market demand and contract clauses incentivize suppliers to redesign products, substitute materials, decarbonize manufacturing energy, reduce waste, and optimize transport—reducing energy- and process-related emissions across the value chain.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower life-cycle GHG emissions associated with purchased goods/services (often primarily Scope 3 for the buyer), contributing to overall emissions reductions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reducing upstream (supply-chain/Scope 3) GHG emissions by shifting purchasing and contracts toward lower-carbon goods and services.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Procurement standards shift demand and impose requirements that reduce upstream production and logistics emissions, yielding net GHG reductions (mitigation).  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although procurement can improve resource efficiency (buying less, circularity) and sometimes nature-based outcomes (e.g., sustainable timber) or resilience (e.g., climate-ready materials), the mechanism as stated is principally about lowering environmental impact/supply-chain emissions through purchasing—best categorized as Mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a procurement standard is exclusively about waste minimization/circularity without clear GHG intent, Resource Efficiency could dominate; if it focuses on climate-risk performance standards (e.g., flood-proofing) it could be Adaptation; if it mandates nature-positive sourcing primarily for carbon sequestration, it could be Nature-Based Solutions. Those are variants not implied by this canonical mechanism focused on emissions reduction.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy whose core mechanism is adopting sustainable procurement standards to shift purchasing toward lower-impact options will, by design, primarily aim to reduce life-cycle/supply-chain emissions; thus Mitigation is consistently the primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific subtypes (e.g., embodied carbon in construction, clean vehicles, renewable electricity procurement, toxics) require policy-level details and are not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some sustainable procurement programs bundle non-climate goals (equity, labor, toxics) and may also reduce emissions via reduced material throughput (resource efficiency); classification remains Mitigation here because the canonical mechanism explicitly targets supply-chain emissions reduction.
key_indicators: “Public procurement contracts meet sustainable procurement criteria”; “incorporating ecological, social and ethical clauses”; “develop a sustainable procurement strategy”; “monitoring its public procurement contracts”; “trained or made aware”
co_benefits: Improved labor/ethical standards and social outcomes; reduced local environmental impacts (e.g., pollution, waste); potential cost efficiency and better contract performance management.
instance_edge_case_notes: Although “ecological” is broad and not explicitly “GHG/carbon,” the procurement criteria/clauses and strategy clearly function as sustainable/green procurement, aligning with mitigation via Scope 3 life-cycle impacts.
parent_policy_name: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.

policy_statement: Implement Green IT strategy to reduce the carbon footprint of digital activity by 20% compared to 2019.
parent_statement: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.
verbatim_text:
## 5. Technology at the service of climate planning

-  Developing a Green IT strategy to enable the development of a digital business.
- o The City Council's Green IT strategy has reduced the carbon footprint of digital activity by 20% compared to 2019.
extraction_rationale: Quantified emissions/footprint reduction target tied to Green IT strategy.
mechanism_description: The city develops a Green IT strategy that reduces the carbon footprint of digital activity, lowering emissions associated with ICT services and operations.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [hazard, exposure, vulnerab, resilien, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Digital service provision → ↓ electricity/fuel use + (sometimes) ↓ embodied emissions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: The city changes how digital/ICT services are designed, procured, operated, and governed (e.g., efficient software and cloud use, consolidating servers, power management, extending device lifetimes, greener procurement, renewable-powered hosting, reducing unnecessary data/storage/traffic).  
2) Mediating physical/ecological process: Lower compute, storage, and transmission demand reduces electricity consumption in data centers, networks, and devices; procurement/asset strategies can also reduce upstream manufacturing emissions and e-waste handling impacts.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced Scope 2 (electricity-related) and potentially Scope 3 (supply-chain) GHG emissions attributable to digital activity and municipal ICT operations.
dominant_pathway_test: Reducing the energy demand and carbon intensity of ICT services (devices, networks, data centers, and IT operations) cuts GHG emissions from electricity and supply chains.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s main lever is decreasing energy use and/or carbon intensity of ICT, leading directly to lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: Although “Green IT” can improve resource efficiency (less hardware, longer lifetimes), the single biggest climate effect across policies using this mechanism is emissions reduction from electricity and supply-chain impacts—i.e., mitigation.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a “Green IT strategy” focuses only on circularity (reuse/recycling) without material energy/emissions reductions, it could lean Resource Efficiency, but the canonical mechanism explicitly targets digital emissions reduction, keeping Mitigation primary.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy under this mechanism is defined by reducing the carbon footprint of digital activity; that is inherently a mitigation objective via reduced ICT-related emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether benefits are mainly Scope 2 reductions, Scope 3 procurement impacts, or efficiency vs renewable sourcing depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assignment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “Green IT” programs may be framed as efficiency/circular IT (device reuse, e-waste reduction) or resilience (IT continuity), but under this canonical mechanism the defining feature is reducing ICT-related GHG emissions, so Mitigation remains primary.
key_indicators: “Developing a Green IT strategy”; “reduced the carbon footprint of digital activity by 20% compared to 2019”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: reduced energy costs, improved ICT efficiency, reduced e-waste (not explicitly stated).
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy instrument; however, the explicit quantified reduction (“20% compared to 2019”) makes target-setting the better fit based on the provided excerpt.
parent_policy_name: Transforming the public authority (2030 strategic objectives) to strengthen HR capacity, sustainable finance/tax policy, sustainable procurement, climate monitoring, and Green IT.

section_header: Towards shared governance of the Climate Plan
policy_statement: Towards shared governance of the Climate Plan (2030 strategic objectives) including Local Climate Assembly, support for local initiatives, Future Generations Council, and strengthened external collaboration.
verbatim_text:
## 2030 Strategic objectives

## 1. Setting up the Local Climate Assembly

## 2. Support for local initiatives

## 3. Future Generations Council

## 4. Boosting external trade
extraction_rationale: Governance chapter contains multiple numbered strategic objective lists; umbrella for sub-policies.
mechanism_description: Sets up a Local Climate Assembly and a Future Generations Council, supports local initiatives, and boosts external collaboration to strengthen coordination and capacity to deliver the climate plan.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance capacity building → ↑ institutional adaptive capacity → ↓ climate risk (via better planning/coordination and implementation of resilience measures)
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Establishes shared governance bodies (e.g., climate assemblies, future generations councils) and formal partnerships to coordinate actors, align priorities, and support local initiatives under a climate plan.  
2) Mediating process: Improves institutional processes—information-sharing, stakeholder engagement, accountability, cross-sector coordination, access to external expertise/funding, and continuity of decision-making—thereby reducing implementation gaps and enabling faster, more coherent risk management.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Greater adaptive capacity leading to reduced exposure and vulnerability to climate impacts (e.g., heat, flooding) through better-designed and better-executed adaptation actions; mitigation outcomes may occur indirectly but are not the dominant pathway of the mechanism.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is increased institutional adaptive capacity that improves preparedness and responsiveness to climate hazards.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Governance reforms → increased institutional capacity/coordination → improved preparedness and delivery of resilience measures → reduced climate risk.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While these bodies can also accelerate mitigation plan delivery, the mechanism itself is capacity-building and governance for managing climate challenges; the most general, mechanism-inherent climate effect across instances is adaptive capacity rather than guaranteed emissions reductions (Mitigation) or efficiency gains (Resource Efficiency).  
3) Edge cases applied: If a shared-governance body is narrowly mandated to implement emissions-reduction measures with clear, enforceable mitigation instruments, a policy-level classifier might tag mitigation as a secondary outcome; however, the canonical mechanism here remains institutional capacity building.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that establishes shared climate governance bodies/partnerships to strengthen coordination and capacity will, by design, increase institutional adaptive capacity (even if effectiveness varies), making Adaptation the consistent primary category.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Specific adaptation subtypes (e.g., heat risk governance, flood risk governance) or mitigation co-benefits depend on the downstream actions and evidence in the individual policy text and are deferred to policy-level assessment.

-  The City Council is extending the Call for Climate Projects throughout the year, and adapting the types of green grants to meet the climate challenges and speed up the implementation of climate projects in the region.
-  The City Council supports the development of projects in line with the Climate Plan.
-  ... the City Council is raising the profile of the actions carried out by local interested parties.
extraction_rationale: Concrete program funding/support and visibility measures.
mechanism_description: The City Council extends the call for projects year-round and adapts green grants to speed up implementation of climate-plan-aligned projects and raise the profile of local actors’ actions.
primary_causal_pathway: Public finance/program design → ↑ implementation of low-carbon projects → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) Changes: The city expands availability of green grants (e.g., year-round calls) and reduces administrative/financing barriers so more local actors can start projects sooner. (2) Mediating process: Earlier and broader deployment of climate interventions (e.g., efficiency upgrades, renewable installations, low-carbon mobility, waste reduction) displaces higher-emitting technologies and practices. (3) Outcome: Net reduction in community-wide GHG emissions (and potentially co-benefits like improved air quality); climate impact occurs via funded projects’ emissions effects brought forward in time.
dominant_pathway_test: By accelerating and increasing uptake of climate-plan projects, the biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced GHG emissions (or avoided future emissions) from earlier implementation of mitigation measures.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: governance + grants → faster implementation of low-carbon measures → lower GHG emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: although some funded projects could be adaptation or resource efficiency, the mechanism’s primary climate-relevant effect (as a general class) is enabling emissions reductions by accelerating climate-plan-aligned mitigation actions.  
3) Edge cases applied: if a jurisdiction’s green grants are primarily for resilience (e.g., floodproofing) or nature projects (e.g., urban forestry), the dominant effect could shift to Adaptation or Nature-Based Solutions; however that would be a different canonical mechanism (or a differently scoped grants mechanism) defined by the funded project type.  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances sharing this mechanism: any “green grants expansion to accelerate climate project implementation” is an enabling lever whose main climate pathway is accelerated deployment of interventions; across typical climate plans, the largest aggregate effect is emissions reduction.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: whether any given policy instance should be tagged additionally as adaptation/resource efficiency/NBS depends on the funded project portfolio and eligibility rules, which must be assessed at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the grants are not explicitly constrained to mitigation-oriented projects (or are dominated by resilience/nature projects), the primary category could shift; portfolio composition and eligibility criteria are the key evidence needed to resolve this at policy level.

policy_statement: Adopt and implement the City of Brussels Climate Plan targeting at least a 55% reduction in GHG emissions by 2030 (vs. 2008), carbon neutrality by 2050, and energy carbon neutrality for public buildings by 2040.
verbatim_text: Today, inview of the climate change challenges that lie ahead, the City of Brussels is taking action by adopting a new Climate Plan with an even more ambitious long-term objective: a 55%reduction in greenhouse gas emissions by 2030, and carbon neutrality for the region by 2050. This objective of energy carbon neutrality has even been brought forward to 2040 for public buildings.
extraction_rationale: Plan-level quantified targets with timelines (2030/2040/2050) constitute an explicit policy commitment.
mechanism_description: The city is taking action by adopting a Climate Plan that sets greenhouse gas reduction and carbon neutrality objectives, guiding an overall pathway to reduce emissions.
gate_demoted_reason: evidence quote missing one of: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]; evidence quote missing secondary subtype token: [efficien, kwh, kw·h, per capita, ...]
primary_causal_pathway: Policy target-setting + governance → cross-sector decarbonization actions → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human activity/system: The city adopts a binding or guiding community-wide emissions target (e.g., % reduction by a year, net-zero), aligning planning, budgeting, regulations, and investments across energy, buildings, transport, waste, and industry.  
(2) Mediating physical process: Reduced combustion of fossil fuels, improved energy efficiency, fuel switching/electrification, methane reductions, and other sector measures lower the mass flow of GHGs released to the atmosphere.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower community-wide GHG emissions (CO₂e) leading to reduced cumulative atmospheric GHG concentrations and reduced radiative forcing over time.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is achieving economy-wide reductions in greenhouse gas emissions (often to net-zero) across major sectors.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: The mechanism’s central function is to set an economy-wide emissions outcome that triggers and coordinates decarbonization measures, yielding ↓ GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While targets can indirectly improve efficiency or resilience, their defining and largest intended climate effect is emissions mitigation at the community scale, not adaptation (hazard/exposure reduction), not resource efficiency as an end in itself, and not ecosystem-based carbon sequestration as the primary lever.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some targets include “net-zero” via offsets or land-sector sinks; even then, the dominant climate-relevant purpose remains achieving net emissions reductions/neutrality (mitigation). If a document is purely aspirational with no implementation linkage, it is still categorized by intended dominant pathway (mitigation).  
4) Confirm applies to ALL instances: Any policy that sets a community-wide GHG reduction or net-zero target shares the same primary mechanism—economy-wide emissions reduction—regardless of sector mix or local context.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether delivery relies more on efficiency, electrification, renewables, waste methane controls, offsets, or nature-based sinks requires policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level classification.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Targets framed mainly as “climate neutrality” can be met with offsets or external credits; classification remains Mitigation, but downstream interpretation should note whether reductions are in-boundary vs. compensated. Targets limited to municipal operations (not community-wide) would be a different mechanism and should not be conflated.

co_benefits: None
